content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer NNN. Construct a permutation AAA of length NNN which is **attractive**.

A permutation is called **attractive** if the bitwise XOR of all absolute differences of adjacent pairs of elements is equal to 000.

Formally, a permutation A=[A1,A2,…,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N]A=[A1​,A2​,…,AN​] of length NNN is said to be attractive if:

∣A1−A2∣⊕∣A2−A3∣⊕…⊕∣AN−1−AN∣=0|A_{1}-A_{2}|  \oplus |A_{2}-A_{3}| \oplus \ldots \oplus |A_{N-1} - A_{N}| = 0∣A1​−A2​∣⊕∣A2​−A3​∣⊕…⊕∣AN−1​−AN​∣=0

where ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operation.

Output **any** attractive permutation of length NNN. If no attractive permutation exists, print −1-1−1 instead.

**Note:** A permutation of length NNN is an array A=[A1,A2,…,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N]A=[A1​,A2​,…,AN​] such that every integer from 111 to NNN occurs exactly once in AAA. For example, [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3] and [2,3,1][2, 3, 1][2,3,1] are permutations of length 333, but [1,2,1][1, 2, 1][1,2,1], [4,1,2][4, 1, 2][4,1,2], and [2,3,1,4][2, 3, 1, 4][2,3,1,4] are not.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing one integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a single line an attractive permutation of NNN integers, or −1-1−1 if no attractive permutation exists.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line containing N space‑separated integers – an attractive permutation of length N – or -1 if no such permutation exists.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=3 (odd) the identity permutation works: differences are 1 and 1, XOR=0. For N=6 (even) the construction 1 6 2 3 4 5 gives differences 5,4,1,1,1 whose XOR is 0.
N=2 is the only impossible size. N=4 (even) uses 1 4 2 3, differences 3,2,1 → XOR=0. N=5 (odd) uses the identity permutation.

solution.approach:
The XOR of the absolute differences of adjacent elements must be zero. Observe that the identity permutation [1,2,…,N] gives N‑1 differences equal to 1. Their XOR is 0 when N‑1 is even, i.e. when N is odd. Hence for every odd N the identity permutation is attractive. For even N we need a different construction. The permutation
    1, N, 2, 3, 4, …, N‑1
has differences |1‑N| = N‑1, |N‑2| = N‑2 and then N‑3 differences equal to 1. The XOR of the first two differences is (N‑1) xor (N‑2) = 1. Since N is even, N‑3 is odd, so XORing an odd number of 1’s flips the result to 0. This works for every even N ≥ 4. The only impossible case is N = 2, because the only two permutations give a single difference of 1, whose XOR is 1. The algorithm therefore is:
1. If N == 2 → output -1.
2. Else if N is odd → output 1 2 … N.
3. Else (N even) → output 1 N 2 3 … N‑1.
The construction is linear in N and uses O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(ΣN) ≤ 2·10^5.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) auxiliary space (output array not counted).
